title: English muffins
subtitle: Yeasted griddle breads with an open, spongy crumb
excerpt: Soft yeasted rounds cooked slowly on a flat griddle rather than in the oven, giving a pale exterior and a spongy, holey interior. Fork-split to serve, which preserves the nooks that catch the butter. The characteristic holes form during the slow griddle cook.
sourceNotes: English muffins as a griddle-cooked yeasted bread are documented in 19th-century British baking literature. Mrs Beeton, Book of Household Management (1861), Project Gutenberg #10136, includes a recipe for 'muffins' cooked on a bakestone, enriched with milk. Eliza Acton, Modern Cookery for Private Families (1845), Project Gutenberg, also records a similar muffin baked on an iron plate. The recipe here follows the classic milk-enriched griddle tradition rather than the baking-powder American muffin, which is a different product entirely.

content.text: English muffins are a griddle bread, not a baked one. The low, slow heat on a heavy pan sets the crumb from outside to centre without the oven's direct heat, which produces the soft, pale crust and the spongy, holey interior. Always
rather than cutting: the torn surface toasts better and holds more butter. The dough is wetter than most breads, which creates the holes.

Combine flour, yeast, sugar, and salt in a large bowl. Pour in the warm milk, water, and melted butter. Mix to a soft, slightly sticky dough (wetter than a standard bread dough). Knead for 8 to 10 minutes by hand until smooth and elastic. The dough will remain tacky; this is correct. Shape into a ball, return to the bowl, cover, and leave to rise for 60 minutes until doubled in size.

Dust the work surface generously with semolina. Roll the dough out to about 2.5 cm thick. Cut rounds with a 9 cm cutter, pressing straight down without twisting. Place on semolina-dusted baking paper. Re-roll the offcuts once only. Cover loosely and prove for 30 minutes.

Heat a heavy cast-iron skillet or frying pan over a low-medium heat. No oil. Cook the muffins in batches of 3 to 4 (don't crowd the pan), for 8 minutes per side. The muffins should look pale gold, not browned. The surface will look dry when it is time to flip. They are done when both sides are gold and the muffin feels set to a gentle press on the side. Cool on a rack before splitting.
